How can Indigenous people  and local communities help  safeguard nature? "

The researchers reviewed an extensive body of literature to understand how Indigenous peoples and local communities (IPLCs)<sup>1</sup> contribute to caring for nature and solving environmental problems around the world. They found that knowledge and practices of IPLCs are making significant contributions towards addressing current environmental crises
column2: such as climate change and biodiversity loss. Specifically

column1: 1. IPLCs manage lands through long-standing local practices. For example
column6: which sets aside parts of rivers as no-fishing or seasonal-fishing zones. The system helped increase fish populations and later received government recognition.

column1: 2. IPLCs conserve and restore ecosystems. For example
column3: the Gunditjmara community at Budj Bim in southwest Victoria
column5: built water channels and ponds to farm eels
column6: maintaining aquatic habitats and supporting local biodiversity. The Budj Bim Cultural Landscape is now recognised as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

column1: 3. IPLCs produce knowledge for assessing and monitoring ecosystems. For example
column2: there are 746 documented climate change indicators developed by IPLCs

column1: 4. IPLCs resist resource-extracting industries and defend their lands. For example
column2: the Shipibo-Conibo community in the Peruvian Amazon resisted a palm oil company that illegally deforested 7
column3: 000 hectares of land in their region. They took legal action
column4: filed complaints with international bodies and mobilised global support

column1: 5. IPLCs contribute to global environmental policy development. For example
column2: IPLCs have influenced decision-making on climate change
column3: such as through the Local Communities and Indigenous Peoples Platform within the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change
column4: a formal mechanism that allows them to share knowledge
column5: participate in discussions and help shape global climate policies.

column1: 6. IPLCs offer alternative concepts/philosophies of relations between people and nature.  For example
column2: Māori groups in New Zealand/Aotearoa secured the legal recognition of the Whanganui River with rights of personhood
column3: in line with their view of the river as a living entity
column4: which ensured stronger protection to the river.

column1: The knowledge and practices of IPLCs have not been fully recognised in policies for environmental conservation and sustainable development
column2: where scientific and technical approaches continue to dominate. This study presents one of the first comprehensive syntheses of evidence clearly demonstrating the important contributions of IPLCs to addressing today’s environmental crises.

column1: The study suggests that IPLCs and their governance systems should be recognised as key partners and institutions in nature conservation and the sustainable use of natural resources. To ensure justice and equity in nature conservation
column2: it is necessary to respect Indigenous rights
column3: including secure land tenure and free
column4: prior and informed consent. Collaborative approaches
column5: in which IPLCs and scientists co-produce knowledge by combining traditional practices with scientific methods
column6: can improve global understanding of environmental changes and support decisions that protect both people and nature.

Why cancer immunotherapy helps some patients more than others?  "
The researchers examined many scientific studies to better understand how microRNAs
column2: tiny molecules inside cells that control how genes work
column3: affect cancer and the immune system
column4: and what this means for treatment. They found that different microRNAs can have very different effects: some help cancer survive by preventing the immune system from noticing or responding to the tumor
column5: while others make the immune system more alert and active against cancer. They also identified microRNAs that may help explain why some patients can benefit particularly well from immunotherapy – a new treatment that trains the body’s immune system to recognise and attack cancer cells
column6: with fewer side effects than traditional chemotherapy or radiotherapy

column1: Immunotherapy works for some patients but not for others
column2: and it is unclear why. MicroRNAs can influence both cancer cells and the immune system
column3: so understanding them could help explain these differences. The study is important because it highlights microRNAs’ potential as signals to guide treatment decisions.

column2: researchers could explore ways to target microRNAs to make immunotherapy more effective against cancer. Doctors could use microRNAs to predict which patients are most likely to benefit from cancer immunotherapy. Patients could eventually receive more personalised and effective treatments with fewer side effects
column3: along with a clearer understanding of how information from tests that measure microRNA levels guides their doctor’s treatment decisions.
